text:
In this paper, we propose a quantum algorithm for support vector machine (SVM) (one of the most famous supervised learning algorithms in classical machine learning for binary classification). By utilizing characteristics of quantum computing, the time complexity of the corresponding quantum algorithm is shown to be logN and logM. In other words, the proposed quantum algorithm can reduce the computational complexity from the polynomial time level in the classical case to logarithmic time level. For example, when the dimensionality of the data in the feature space is high (such as, e.g., image recognition), the data points can no longer be stored in the main memory, leading to a high polynomial time complexity for classical algorithms. This limits the corresponding classical SVM algorithm to process data with moderate dimensions. When the size of the data is very large, the classical SVM algorithm also runs for a long time, which severely limits its application to the analysis of large-scale data.

This “big data” algorithm is based on a new technical representation of Support Vector Machines (SVMs) as a least-squares problem whose solution is given by the quantum matrix inversion algorithm. At the heart of the algorithm is an innovation for the exponentiation of non-sparse matrices (i.e. dense matrices with nonzero entries almost everywhere) that allows one to transform the kernel matrix of the training data in an efficient manner, similar to a quantum principal component analysis, which yields the principal features of the data set while being computationally efficient. The method can also be used for the non-linear SVM realizable by means of polynomial kernels in the ambient tensor product space of sufficiently large finite dimension. One more benefit of the quantum SVM in comparison to its classical counterparts is given by the data privacy property that the statistical information from the training set encoded in the kernel matrix of the data is processed in the quantum algorithm without explicitly disclosing the individual samples in the training set.
